name: Steven Jones Thermite
description: The Steven Jones Thermite theory, a prominent element of the 9/11 conspiracy theories, centers on the collapse of the World Trade Center towers. This theory, popularized by physicist Steven Jones, posits that explosives, specifically thermite or thermate (a variant of thermite with added sulfur), were secretly planted in the towers and detonated to cause their controlled demolition. Proponents argue that the official explanation – structural failure due to fire – is insufficient to account for the speed and nature of the collapses. They suggest that the presence of molten metal found in the debris, and the discovery of red-gray chips containing unreacted thermitic material, are evidence of a pre-planned demolition. Mainstream scientific and engineering consensus, represented by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) report on the WTC collapses, refutes the thermite theory. NIST concluded that the collapses were initiated by the impact and subsequent fires, which weakened the steel support structures. They attribute the molten metal to the burning of materials within the buildings (aluminum, etc.) and the red-gray chips to ordinary epoxy coatings and primer paint containing iron oxide particles. The NIST report and other scientific analyses have also challenged the claims regarding the speed and symmetry of the collapses, demonstrating how progressive failure can lead to a rapid and seemingly controlled descent. The thermite theory remains popular among 9/11 truthers, despite the lack of conclusive scientific support and the overwhelming evidence supporting the official explanation.
subtitle: 9/11 conspiracy theory involving explosives

claims.claim: Thermite or thermate was used to weaken the steel structures of the WTC towers.
claims.evidence: Discovery of red-gray chips containing iron oxide and aluminum, suggestive of thermitic material, in WTC dust samples.
claims.criticism: NIST analysis identified the chips as paint primer and epoxy, containing iron oxide, commonly used in construction. Independent analyses have also questioned the chain of custody and potential contamination of the samples.

claims.claim: Molten metal observed in the debris of the WTC towers is evidence of thermite reactions.
claims.evidence: Witness accounts and photographic evidence of molten metal flowing from the towers before and after the collapse.
claims.criticism: NIST attributed the molten metal to the burning of aluminum and other materials present in the buildings, combined with the high temperatures generated by the fires. The amount of thermite needed to melt the observed quantities of steel would have been enormous and readily detectable.

claims.claim: The rapid and symmetrical collapse of the towers indicates controlled demolition.
claims.evidence: The towers fell almost straight down at near free-fall speed, suggesting that the steel supports were systematically weakened or severed.
claims.criticism: NIST's simulations and analyses demonstrate that progressive failure, caused by the weakening of steel columns due to fire and impact damage, can explain the speed and symmetry of the collapses. The collapses were not perfectly symmetrical, and the speed was not truly free-fall.

keyFigures.name: Steven Jones
keyFigures.role: Physicist and leading proponent of the thermite theory.

keyFigures.name: Frank Legge
keyFigures.role: Chemist and co-author of papers supporting the thermite theory.

keyFigures.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
keyFigures.role: Conducted the official investigation into the WTC collapses and refuted the thermite theory.

mythologyConnections: None explicitly related, but can be seen as a modern myth relating to power, deception, and hidden truths.

timeline.event: Steven Jones publishes a paper suggesting the use of thermite in the WTC collapses.
Jones and others publish a paper in The Open Chemical Physics Journal presenting evidence of thermitic material in WTC dust.
Jones is placed on administrative leave from Brigham Young University and later resigns.
Numerous scientific rebuttals and analyses refute the thermite theory.

sources.author: Steven E. Jones, et al.
sources.work: Active Thermitic Material Discovered in Dust from the 9/11 World Trade Center Catastrophe
sources.title: Steven E. Jones, et al.
